channel.title: Seoul on paper.archive
channel.description: Recent content in Seoul on paper.archive

item.title: Korean Shabu-Shabu at Home — the Three-Act Dinner
item.description:
Tonight’s dinner: Deungchon-style shabu-shabu, delivered — with one hack. The beef came from the supermarket, since a tray of shabu-sliced beef costs less than the delivery add-on and you get more of it.

What is Korean shabu-shabu? Korea took the Japanese swish-swish hot pot and made it its own — a spicy, deeply seasoned broth loaded with mushrooms, minari (water dropwort), and bean sprouts. “Deungchon shabu” is a beloved Seoul style built around that fiery broth.

item.title: Kkot-Samgyeop (Flower Pork Belly) — Daebit Sikdang, Gaebong-dong
item.description:
Korean BBQ night at Daebit Sikdang near home. Their thing is kkot-samgyeop (꽃삼겹, “flower pork belly”) grilled on a dome-shaped stone pan.

Kkot-samgyeop is thin-sliced pork belly rolled up like a rose. It goes on the hot stone dome together with kimchi and mountain vegetables — gosari (fernbrake) and minari (water dropwort).

The dome shape drains the fat away as it cooks, so the pork crisps up without swimming in grease. Wrap it with the greens (ssam style) and it never gets heavy. There’s a little pot of sauce bubbling in the center for dipping.

item.title: Homesul Night — Kahlúa Milk, Jjukkumi, Yukhoe
item.description:
Drinking at home tonight — Koreans call it homesul (홈술, home + sul/alcohol). All the food is takeout.

The drink: Kahlúa mixed with milk. Not Korean at all, but it’s a beloved “easy mode” drink here for nights when soju feels like too much.

The anju (drinking snacks):

  • Jjukkumi-bokkeum (쭈꾸미볶음) — stir-fried baby octopus in a spicy gochujang sauce. The heat plays surprisingly well against the sweet, milky Kahlúa
  • Yukhoe (육회) — Korean-style beef tartare, seasoned with sesame oil. Nutty, chewy, needs no explanation

Takeout containers, comfortable clothes, something on the TV. Homesul has its own charm.

item.title: Dakjang Maekju — My Neighborhood Chicken & Beer Pub
item.description:
My local pub, Dakjang Maekju in Gaebong-dong — walking distance from home, which in Korea is a blessing. The whole “chicken and beer” culture here even has its own word: chimaek (치맥, chicken + maekju/beer).

Tonight’s order: dak-bokkeum-tang (닭볶음탕 — spicy braised chicken stew) with wang-daechang (beef large intestine) added, ₩32,000. Chicken, potatoes, and rice cakes simmered in a gochujang broth right at the table. You pick the spice level; we went medium. The potatoes soak up the broth and might be the best part.

item.title: Gopchang Jeongol — Korean Intestine Hot Pot in Gaebong-dong
item.description:
Tonight’s anju (안주 — food you eat while drinking) is gopchang jeongol, at my regular place Yeokjeon Sundae-guk in Gaebong-dong, Seoul.

What is it? Gopchang jeongol (곱창전골) is a spicy hot pot loaded with beef or pork intestines, simmered at your table in a gochujang-based broth. Sounds intense if you’ve never tried offal, but done right the gopchang is chewy, clean-tasting, and the broth gets deeper the longer it boils.
